title: Providing for consideration of the bill (H.R. 1101) to prohibit unlawful access to the payment system of the Bureau of the Fiscal Service within the Department of the Treasury, and for other purposes.
plain_summary: This resolution creates special rules for Congress to quickly vote on a bill about protecting Treasury payment systems. The bill they want to vote on would make it illegal to break into the government's money payment computers. Instead of following normal long procedures, they will only debate for one hour before voting. If the bill passes, they have to tell the Senate about it within one week.
last_action_text: Motion to Discharge Committee filed by Mr. Casten. Petition No: 119-2. (Discharge petition text with signatures.)

sections.title: Resolution Text
sections.text: The House of Representatives will immediately consider bill H.R. 1101 when this resolution passes. H.R. 1101 is a bill that would make it illegal to unlawfully access the payment system of the Bureau of the Fiscal Service, which is part of the Department of the Treasury. Any rules that might normally prevent the House from considering this bill are set aside. The bill will be treated as if it has already been read out loud. Any rules that might challenge specific parts of the bill are also set aside. The House will move straight to a final vote on the bill after allowing one hour of debate. During this debate time, the chair and the top minority member of the Committee on Financial Services will each control thirty minutes, or they can let someone else control their time. After the debate, members will have one chance to send the bill back to committee with changes before the final vote.

sections.title: Section 2 Rule Exceptions
sections.text: Two specific House rules will not apply when considering H.R. 1101. These are Clause 1(c) of rule XIX and clause 8 of rule XX. These rules normally require certain procedures that will be skipped for this bill.

sections.title: Section 3 Senate Notification
sections.text: The House Clerk must send a message to the Senate within one week after H.R. 1101 passes. This message will tell the Senate that the House has passed the bill.
